title: Equity, poverty & income distribution
summary:
## Equity vs equality vs efficiency

- **Equality** means everyone has the *same*; **equity** means *fairness*, which may not mean equal shares.
- **Horizontal equity** — treating those in the same situation the same.
- **Vertical equity** — treating differently those in different situations (e.g. the rich pay more tax).
Governments often face an **equity–efficiency trade-off**: redistribution can blunt incentives to work, save and take risks, while too much inequality has social and economic costs.

## Measuring income and wealth inequality

- **Income** is a *flow* (earnings, benefits over time); **wealth** is a *stock* (assets owned). Wealth is usually **more unequally distributed** than income.
- **The Lorenz curve** plots the cumulative % of income against the cumulative % of population; the further it bows from the 45° **line of equality**, the greater the inequality.
- **The Gini coefficient** = area between the Lorenz curve and the line of equality ÷ total area under the line. It ranges from **0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality)** — a single summary number.

## Poverty

- **Absolute poverty** — income below a level needed for **basic necessities** (e.g. the World Bank's international poverty line). Falls with growth.
- **Relative poverty** — income **below a set proportion (e.g. 60%) of the median** in that society; a measure of inequality that can persist even as a country grows.
- **Causes:** unemployment, low wages/skills, illness/age, poor education, discrimination, and the **poverty trap/cycle** (where earning more loses benefits, weakening incentives).

## Policies to reduce poverty and inequality

- **Progressive taxation** — higher earners pay a larger *proportion* (contrast **regressive** taxes like VAT that hit the poor harder, and **proportional** flat taxes).
- **Transfer payments / benefits** — pensions, unemployment and child benefits, targeted (means-tested) or universal.
- **State provision of merit goods** — free education and healthcare improve opportunity and human capital.
- **Minimum wages** and labour-market policy.
- **Supply-side measures** — education, training and job creation to tackle the *causes*.

**Evaluation:** redistribution can reduce the **incentive to work** (poverty/unemployment traps), is costly, and may cause government failure; means-testing has admin costs and low take-up. But extreme inequality harms cohesion, health and even growth. The balance is a **normative judgement**.

## Why it matters

Equity is one of the six macro objectives and a recurring evaluation theme. The Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient are examinable tools, and the equity–efficiency trade-off runs through tax, welfare and development policy.

keyTerms.definition: Equity is fairness (which may be unequal); equality is everyone having the same.
Treating people in identical circumstances in the same way.
Treating people in different circumstances differently (e.g. the rich pay more tax).
A graph of cumulative income share against cumulative population share, showing inequality.
A 0–1 measure of inequality; 0 is perfect equality, 1 is perfect inequality.
Income too low to afford basic necessities.
Income below a set proportion of the median in a society.
A tax taking a larger proportion of income as income rises.
When earning more income leads to lost benefits/higher tax, weakening the incentive to work.

diagrams: **Lorenz curve:** the 45° line of equality and a bowed Lorenz curve; the more it bows, the greater the inequality; relate the gap to the Gini coefficient.
Optional: a tax-incidence sketch contrasting **progressive, proportional and regressive** taxes.
tips: Distinguish **income (flow) from wealth (stock)** and note wealth is usually more unequal.
Explain the **Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient** precisely (0–1 scale) — common data-response tools.
Separate **absolute vs relative poverty** and note relative poverty can persist despite growth.
Frame answers around the **equity–efficiency trade-off** and the **poverty/unemployment trap** for evaluation.
Classify policies as tackling **symptoms (transfers) vs causes (education/supply-side)** — a strong structuring point.

channels.why: Clear on inequality, Lorenz/Gini and poverty policy.

keyConcepts.title: Poverty and policies to reduce it
